entry.title: Segmentation-Aware CNN With Local Attention Mask
entry.content:
Segmentation-Aware Convolutional Networks Using Local Attention Masks

  • Adam W. Harley, Konstantinos G. Derpanis, Iasonas Kokkinos
  • ICCV 2017
  • https://github.com/aharley/segaware

Abstract

  • integrate segmentation information:

  • This counter-acts the tendency of CNNs to smooth information across regions and increases their spatial precision.

  • A CNN to provide an embedding space => compute a local attention mask relative to each neuron
  • Segmentation-aware convolution
  • dense prediction tasks
  • semantic segmentation: match performance of DenseCRF but faster and simpler
  • optical flow： sharper response

Introduction

entry.title: Faster R-CNN
entry.content:
Faster R-CNN: Towards Real-Time Object Detection with Region Proposal Networks

Based on Fast R-CNN as detector, Faster R-CNN incorporate proposal generation stage into net by sharing conv features with detector and sliding a mini-subnet over the feat maps. The computation time for porposals is sharply reduced and the proposals’ quality is high.

  • Shaoqing Ren, Kaiming He, Ross Girshick, Jian Sun
  • Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems(NIPS), 2015
  • https://arxiv.org/abs/1506.01497
  • https://github.com/rbgirshick/py-faster-rcnn
  • https://github.com/ShaoqingRen/faster_rcnn

Abstract

  1. Introduce Region Proposal Network(RPN)
    • SPPNet, Fast R-CNN rely on external region proposal algorithms: slow.
    • RPN shares the fully convolutional features with the detection net.
      • cost-free region proposals.
      • predict object bounds and objectness scores at each position.
  2. Results
    • VGG-16
    • 73.2% voc2007; 70.4% voc2014: 300 proposals
    • 5fps

Introduction

  1. Sharing convolution cross proposals: R-CNN -> Fast R-CNN
    • less time
    • computational bottleneck lies in region proposal
  2. RPN share features with object detection network.
    • On top of shared feature map:
      • extract feature vector
      • predict objectness and regression for k proposals relative to k anchors of various scales and radios at a location

Apporach

Region Proposal Networks

  • Sliding window (size \(3*3\)) -> \(3*3\) conv -> two \(1*1\) conv
  • 9 anchors at one position: 3 scales, 3 aspect radios, 4k outputs for encoding coords, 2k scores. Total \(kHW\) anchors for conv feat map of size \(H*W\).
  • traslation invariant: address the problems of obj translation (because it is convolutional.): less paramters compared to MultiBox.

Leaning Region Proposal

  • Positive samples:
    • the highest IoU overlap with a gt bbox
    • IoU overlap with a gt bbox is higher than 0.7
  • Negative samples:
    • IoU overlaps with all gt bboxes are less than 0.3

  • Others: No contrib

  • Loss
    • \(*\) means gt. \(p_i^*\) is either 0 or 1 (gt labels: neg/pos).
    • \(t\) is a encoding of coords (\(x,y,h,w\), center coords and sizes) with respect to anchors.
    • reg loss is only activated for pos anchor.

  • Learnable weights: NOT SHARED for k anchors: one for each (scale, shape).

Optimization

  • randomly sample 256 rois for rpn loss in a mini-batch.
  • for sampled rois, pos:neg = 1:1, pad pos if less pos.
  • fix some paramters to conserve memory.
  • Sharing convolutional features
  • complex 4-step training algo

Implementation details

  • resize image: shorter side to 600
  • total strides of last conv feature in VGG/ZF : 16.
  • 3 scales: \(128^2,256^2,512^2\) (8,16,32 relative to the stride (in the feature map))
  • 3 aspect radios: \(1:1,1:2,2:1\)
  • \(1000*600\) image: \(60*40*9\) anchors
  • ignore boundary boxxes during training
  • hard to train.
  • 6k anchors left.
  • NOT in testing.
  • RPN NMS
  • IoU threshold: 0.7.
  • 2k proposals for training Fast R-CNN.
  • top-N (300 is good) ranked used for detection in test.
  • Those proposal are transformed by their predictions to rois as regions for feature extraction in Fast RCNN.

Experiments

  • save time in test:
  • shared features

  • less proposals for detection: reduct the roi-wise fc cost

  • pay attetion to table 4.

It is noteworthy that the Recall-to-IoU metric is just loosely related to the ultimate detection accuracy.

Note perfomance drops more quickly for SS & EB than RPN when proposals are fewer.

Other things

  1. Why it is called region-base cnn? In my understanding, this is because since its first generation, in the network, there are operations are region-based(or roi-wise). This is inherent in object detection tasks.

  2. alternative finetuning or train jointly?

  3. It is not clear a priori if learning Fast R-CNN while simultaneously changing the proposal mechanism will converge.

  4. We note that our algorithm allows the use of anchor boxes that are larger than the underlying receptive field when predicting large proposals. Such predictions are not impossible, one may still roughly infer the extent of an object if only the middle of the object is visible. I still think there might be improvement.

  5. It is noteworthy that the Recall-to-IoU metric is just loosely related to the ultimate detection accuracy. It is more appropriate to use this metric to diagnose the proposal method than to evaluate it.

  6. Details about Faster R-CNN with Resnets Backbone.
entry.summary: <h1 id="faster-r-cnn-towards-real-time-object-detection-with-region-proposal-networks">Faster R-CNN: Towards Real-Time Object Detection with

entry.title: FPN
entry.content:
Feature Pyramid Networks for Object Detection

  • Tsung-Yi Lin, Piotr Dollar, Ross Girshick, Kaiming He, Bharath Hariharan, Serge Belongie
  • CVPR 2017
  • https://arxiv.org/abs/1612.03144

Abstract

  • Feature Pyramid
  • exploit inherent multi-scale, pyramidal hierarchy of DCN: marginal extra cost.
  • bottom-up (CNN ifself) + top-down(high-level semantic information) + lateral connection(for information fusion)
  • detect objects of different scales.
  • Results
  • Use on Faster R-CNN > other single model
  • 5fps

Introduction

  • Feature Pyramid:
  • standard solution: image pyramid
  • scale-invariant: object scale change = shift its level in pyramid
  • applied in testing generally: mutli-scale images as input => all levels, including high resolution is semantically strong
  • limitation: inference time increase + end-to-end training is infeasible + inconsistency between training and testing inference
  • Exploit inherent CNN structure:
  • high resolution <-> low semantic
  • SSD doesn’t reuse high resolution feat maps, which is important for small object detection
  • FPN = bottom-up + top-down + lateral
    • proposals: AR +8%
    • detection: COCO AP +2.3%; VOC AP+3.8% > single-scale Faster R-CNN with ResNet
    • segmentation

Approach

Structure

  • Bottom-up
  • stage: layers that output feat maps of the same size.
  • last layers of stages to construct pyramid
  • Resnet: {C2,C3,C4,C5}=>stride {4,8,16,32}, C1 discarded.
  • Top-down path + lateral connection
  • add 1*1 conv on C5 => coarsest feat map for starting iter
  • {C2,C3,C4,C5} => merged feats
  • merged feats + conv \(3*3\) => final feats {P2,P3,P4,P5}:
    • conv 3*3
    • reduce aliasing effect of upsampling
    • output channels: 256-d, for shared classifier and regressor
    • no nonlinearity

Applications

FPN for RPN

  • original RPN:
  • head
  • anchor
  • on single-scale feature map
  • Adapted version:
  • on each level of pyramid
  • each level deal with one size of anchors.
    • {\(32^2,64^2,128^2,256^2,512^2\)} on {P2,P3,P4,P5,P6}
    • P6: Stride 2 upsampling of P5, only introduced for the \(512^2\) scale, not applied in Fast R-CNN detector
    • radios: still {\(1:1,1:2,2:1\)}
    • 15 kinds of anchors
    • Implementation: the same as original one, see Faster R-CNN
    • No need to explicitly assign GT bboxes to different levels

  • we have also evaluated the alternative without sharing parameters and observed similar accuracy. The good performance of sharing parameters indicates that all levels of our pyramid share similar semantic levels. This advantage is analogous to that of using a featurized image pyramid, where a common head classifier can be applied to features computed at any image scale.

FPN for Fast R-CNN

  • assign RoIs of diff scals to diff levels
  • roi (w,h) -> \(P_k\): \[k=\lfloor k_0+log_2(\sqrt{wh}/224) \rfloor\] > Here 224 is the canonical ImageNet pre-training size, and k0 is the target level on which an RoI with w * h = 224 * 224 should be mapped into.
  • \(k_0=4\)
  • Diff from original Fast R-CNN with ResNets
  • original
    • conv5 (9-layer sub-network) adopted as the head on top of conv4 feats
  • In this paper:
    • conv5 used for pyramid
    • share heads: 7*7 roi-pooling + 2 1024-d fc layers with relu + final classification and bbox regression layers.
    • lighter weight and faster

Experiments

  • coco: 25k trainval + 5k minival

RPN

  • AR

Pay special attention to the improvements of small and middle-size objs.

Fast/Faster R-CNN

Fast R-CNN (on fixed proposals)

  • use proposals from RPN Table 1-(c) (fixed)

  • Note the lighter 2-fc head used in this model and meaning of conducting experiments (b).
  • The problems of exp (f)

  • We argue that this is because RoI pooling is a warping-like operation, which is less sensitive to the region’s scales.

  • Note high level top down information is still accessible in P2

Faster R-CNN (on consistent proposals)

* Note why the reproduction is much bettter: more anchors; more RoIs; larger images … * Even tough extra layers, head is lighter: 0.20-0.24s vs 0.32 (Faster R-CNN)

Comparing with COCO winners

  • note only single scale input
  • potential imporvements:
  • iterative regression
  • hard negative mining
  • context modeling
  • stronger data augmentation

Segmentation

to do.

others

  1. region-based detectors are detectors that don’t use sliding windows. Generally, region-based detectors are two-stage detectors. They need select rois from proposals, which are generated by sliding windows.

  2. The num of ROIs in Fast/Faster R-CNN

  3. The problem of exp (f) in Fast R-CNN

  4. Why 4-step training in Faster R-CNN?

  5. We find that our ResNet-101 model in Table 5 is not sufficiently trained (?) with the default learning rate schedule. So we increase the number of mini-batches by 2× at each learning rate when training the Fast R-CNN step.
entry.summary:
Feature Pyramid Networks for Object Detection

  • Tsung-Yi Lin, Piotr Dol

entry.title: Mxnet Tips
entry.content:
• How to retrive all the weights in the network?

see fcis/lib/utils/symbol.py:Symbol.check_parameter_shapes

Using sym.list_arguments() can help us get all the arguments from a network, which include learnable weights, data and label. A straight forward way is to eliminate data and label by their names.

Of course, we can use the utility provided by fcis.

  • It is important to have each layer named. It is useful for:
  • initialization for specific layers

  • understanding debug information (the name of layer may be available in the debug information.)

  • How to vizualize network? use function mx.viz.plot_network(sym,...).

  • How to debug?
  1. If we want to know the statistics of learnable weights or their gradients.

    1. use mx.mon.Monitor.

      • https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/monitor.py
      • https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/example/python-howto/monitor_weights.py

      The only difficulty here is how to use pattern to match the names of weights, data or output. See problem How does the naming system in mxnet work?. The easiest way to print all the stuff associated with a layer and a layer can be easily found by its name.

      We should name our each layer in a systematic and elegant way so that we can find them easily.

    2. use custom Print operator.

      This is easy to use and flexible, but you have to write the custom operators and insert it in your network. In the implementation, you just need to print input and copy input to output.

  • How does the naming system in mxnet work?

A network is composed of data blobs and layers. In the usual case, we can name layers at our will, e.g. name='FC_Layer' or it will get default name, e.g. fc1. The default name start counting at 1. It can be read from [sym].name.

Blobs are associated with layers, generally including input, output and weights. Because input/output data blob would be shared by multiple layers, the name of input data blob is specified/overwriten by the lower layer (the name of output data blob).

The output blob would be named as [layer_name]_[output_name], output_name is by default output[id] (possibably multiple outputs). We can get output_name by .list_outputs() method.

The paramter blob would be named as [layer_name]_[default_paramter_name], e.g. fc1_weight and fc1_bias. These rules also apply to our self-defined layers. If we provide parameters by a Variable, the name would be overwriten by the name of the Variable. This is consistent with the case when we input other data blobs into this layer. We can read parameter_name from .list_arguments(). But it is still not so straightforward, because .list_arguments() list all the arguments that is required in the whole computation graph rather than this node.

So, how about gradients of those blob? Their names seems purely defined as [layer_name]_backward_[default_blob_name], such as fc1_backward_weight, fc1_backward_bias, fc1_backward_data, even if input data blob actually has name data_img for example. Anyway, I really think this is confusing.

  • Note that data/label name provided by your data iter should match the ones required by your network.
entry.summary:
• How to retrive all the weights in the network?

see fcis/lib/utils/symbol.py:Symbol.check_parameter_s

entry.title: R-FCN
entry.content:
R-FCN: Object Detection via Region-based Fully Convolutional Networks

  • Jifeng Dai, Yi Li, Kaiming He, and Jian Sun
  • Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NIPS), 2016
  • https://arxiv.org/abs/1605.06409
  • https://github.com/daijifeng001/r-fcn
  • https://github.com/YuwenXiong/py-R-FCN

Abstract

This paper propose a fully convolutional structure for object detection, which removes the heavy roi-wise operations after roi-pooling. To compensate translation invariance which is original addressed by those roi-wise operations, position-sensitive score maps are introduced. This model saves a lot of time by removing roi-wise headers and sharing computations in fully convolutional way.

  1. Fully Convolution and Position-Sensitive Score Maps for Object Detection

    • Previous region based networks auch as Fast/Faster R-CNN apply a costly per-region subnetworks(those headers for classification and regression) hundreds of times. (Note after roi-pooling, even though we can implement headers in the convolutional way, the computation is no longer shared. Overlaps of rois are repeatedly computed.)

    • The region-based detector in this paper is fully convolutional, and allow all the computation shared on the entire image. This paper proposes position-sensitive score maps to address the dilemma between translation invariance in image classification and traslation variance in object detection. (By translation, we mean an object move relative to its bbox.)

  2. Results:
    • Resnet-101
    • 83.6% map on PASCAL VOC 2007. 82.0% on 2012.
    • 170ms/img in inference: 2.5-20 times faster then Faster R-CNN

Introduction

  1. RoI pooling layer divides previous prevalent deep object detectors into two subnets:
    • A shared convolutional one for feature extraction
    • A RoI-wise subnet don’t share computation

    resulted from old classification system.(final spatial pooling layer -> roi pooling layer)

  2. The state-of-art classification system is fully convolutional, like ResNets. In order to use fully convolutional backbone, we have to solve the aforementioned dilemma. Inserting RoI pooling (a region-specific operation) is a solution but it introduce heavy headers(those headers lead to translation variance). See Table 1.
    • TODO Review Fast R-CNN
    • Examples:
      • Translation of objects should reflect the change of overlap between gt object and bbox.
      • The left hands appering in the right of bbox should be the hands of others.
  3. Structure Comparison
    • fully convolution (output feature maps) -> roi pooling -> roi-wise opertaion (fc or conv)
    • fully convolution (output position-sensitive score maps) -> position-sensitive poooling -> without weighted layer

Approach

  • RPN & R-FCN share features
  • Backbone: ResNet-101 with feature reduction to 1024 channels.
  • ps score maps: \(k^2(C+1)\) channels, \(k^2\) is corespoing to \(k^2\) bin.
  • ps roi pooling: for class c, bin location x,y, selectively pool the corespondent channel. Aggregate scores from \(k^2\) channels.
  • Average \(k^2\) scores as final voting results.
  • Similar class agnostic bbox regresssion.
  • OHEM: online hard example mining.
  • hole algorithm.

Archtecture

### Visualization

Experiments

Results

  • voc 2007/2012
  • coco

Other Things

  1. The inference process is easy to understand. How about the training part? Specifically, how do final score maps learn to be position sensitive? Through end-to-end learning, the successive selective pooling shepherds the convolution layer to learn specialized position-sensitive score maps. This position sensitive score maps may be slightly different from the one in fcis. It only shows the potential a point may belong to a certain part of bbox. So a point didn’t belong to an object but belong to another object near this object may be evaluated as high probability. Even tough proposals may not overlap with the gt bbox in a perfect way (So, some one may lead to false learning of locations, e.g. left-up score map shows high value on the center of an object. Notice gt bboxes are always used in training as addtional proposals), the high probability region marked by those proposals is still reasonable if we think all proposals follows a gaussian like distribution. (The most centered bbox (the gt bbox) always gets the best score.)

Anyway the keypoints here we should understand is how end-to-end learning plays a critical role in this process and forces the layer to learn what we want.

A great thing about machine learning is that if you give the model flexility to learn critical information, it will always imporve. And if you give the model restrictions, it will learn things more easily.

  1. Compared to another kind of two-step dectors, Faster R-CNN, the current design eliminates the heavy header, which saves a lot of time. Actually, in the second step of Faster R-CNN, the computation time is propotional to the number of propsals from the previous RPN subnet, typically 300 bboxes([300,2048(1024),h,w],to check Faster R-CNN paper), which is a really big cost.
entry.summary: <h1 id="r-fcn-object-detection-via-region-based-fully-convolutional-networks">R-FCN: Object Detection via Region-based Fully Convolutional N

entry.title: Paper Reading List 2017
entry.content:
Machine Learning

Computer Vision

Detection and Segmentation

  1. Feature Pyramid Networks for Object Detection

    Tsung-Yi Lin, Piotr Dollár, Ross Girshick, Kaiming He, Bharath Hariharan, and Serge Belongie

    IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR), 2017

    https://arxiv.org/abs/1612.03144

  2. R-FCN: Object Detection via Region-based Fully Convolutional Networks

    Jifeng Dai, Yi Li, Kaiming He, and Jian Sun

    Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NIPS), 2016

    https://arxiv.org/abs/1605.06409

    https://github.com/daijifeng001/r-fcn

  3. Deformable Convolutional Networks

Jifeng Dai, Haozhi Qi, Yuwen Xiong, Yi Li, Guodong Zhang, Han Hu, and Yichen Wei

International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV), 2017.

https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06211

https://github.com/msracver/Deformable-ConvNets

  1. Fully Convolutional Instance-aware Semantic Segmentation

    Yi Li, Haozhi Qi, Jifeng Dai, Xiangyang Ji, and Yichen Wei IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR), 2017. (Spotlight)

    1-st place winner of the COCO 2016 segmentation challenge

    0.24 sec/image test speed (using ResNet-101 net)

    https://arxiv.org/abs/1611.07709

    https://github.com/msracver/FCIS

  2. Mask R-CNN

    Kaiming He, Georgia Gkioxari, Piotr Dollár, and Ross Girshick

    IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV), 2017 (Oral)

    https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06870

  3. Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection

    Tsung-Yi Lin, Priya Goyal, Ross Girshick, Kaiming He, and Piotr Dollár

    IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV), 2017 (Oral)

    https://arxiv.org/abs/1708.02002

  4. Flow-Guided Feature Aggregation for Video Object Detection

    Xizhou Zhu, Yujie Wang, Jifeng Dai, Lu Yuan, and Yichen Wei

    International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV), 2017.

    https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.10025

  5. CoupleNet: Coupling Global Structure with Local Parts for Object Detection

    Yousong Zhu, Chaoyang Zhao, Jinqiao Wang, Xu Zhao, Yi Wu, Hanqing Lu

    Accepted by ICCV 2017

    https://arxiv.org/abs/1708.02863v1

    https://github.com/tshizys/CoupleNet (NA)

  6. Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition

    Kaiming He, Xiangyu Zhang, Shaoqing Ren, Jian Sun

    CVPR 2016

entry.title: Write Custom Operator for Mxent
entry.content:
Implement ROIAlign Operation in Mxnet

Recently, I’m trying to implement ROIAlign operation (reference to mask rcnn) in mxent. I want to write down some keypoints here. Note my version is not official and I copy a lot of codes from github ([ to do: add references]). Anyway, what we focus here is not the specific implementation but some required steps for general cases.

Official Guidances

  1. How to create new operation
  2. Contribute to community: there are links to test cases and examples. Some files that I found useful are:
    1. test_operator.py
    2. test_utils.py
    3. custom_softmax.py
  3. Operator In Mxnet
  4. Source files for operator: Specifically, we may want to take a look at files in contrib folder.
  5. Mshadow: a lightweight CPU/GPU Matrix/Tensor Template Library in C++/CUDA. Just use it as a library when needed.

Specifically, we need to look at the implementation of ROIPoolingOp: roi_pooling-inl.h, roi_pooling.cc, roi_pooling.cu.

GPU Implementation

We focus on GPU implementation here.

First of all there are three files need to write: roi_align-inl.h, roi_align.cc, roi_align.cu.

roi_align-inl.h

An Overview

To do.

Headers and enumerations

  • logging.h: you will find something like CHECK_LT(x, y) and LOG_INFO, which are handy for logging (see google logging).

  • parameter.h: I guess it is used for defining and parsing fields for our operators.

  • operator.h: Our custom operator has to extend Operator class.

  • mshadow_op.h: ? There are matrix operations using mshadow templates. All the operations are in mxnet::op::mshdow_op namespace.

  • operator_common.h: ? .This header includes utility functions operator can use

The following codes are enclosed in mxnet::op namespace. Sincerely speaking, I’m not familiar with namespace. But generally, I think namespace has two effects:

  1. The things you defined are in this namespace.
  2. When use those things outside the namespace, you have to use :: operation.

We actually use those enumerations as indics for input and outputs, e.g. input[roialign::kData], which returns input[0], are actually data input for this operator.

Operator parameters

We define the parameters that are addtional to those blob-like data here using the tools provided in the parameter.h.

Operator operations

Then we define the ROIAlignOp. Its constructor should accept pre-defined Parameter structure as input. explicit prohibits the implicit conversion.

We define virtual Forward function here. The real implementation is located at the final call to ROIAlignForward. Before that call, certain preparetaion are done, including checking, obtaining tensors from in_data and out_data and initialization of tensors. Initialization here requires functions of mashdow, e.g. max_idx_x = -1.0f; set all elements to -1.0 in the matrix. That’s why using namespace mashdow; is required. CHECK_EQs are typical functions in logging system.

Backward here are quite similar to Forward. It also need to do checking, obtain tensors and initialize them. In our case, the initialization depends on the request (to do: I’m not sure req is the abbreviation of this. req[i]`` could be *kNullOp*, *kWriteTo*, *kWriteInplace*, *kAddTo* ... ?). Note grad_in = 0.0f; set all gradients to 0. ROIAlignBackwardAcc undertakes the real task.

An important observation here is that we don’t require all the out_data, out_grad, in_data. For system to recycle memory effectively, we specify these dependencies with the DeclareBackwardDependency interface in OperatorProperty.

About req

I didn’t read the whole document but I guess this term is determined by the engine for the computational graph. For example, if a node bifurcate into two branches, then its gradients need to be accumulated from two sources. Therefore the type of req would be kAddTo in this case. In the normal setting, e.g. sequential connections, kWriteTo should be default.

Factory function

To do.

Operator property

Here is an important separation: * Operator: implementation interface * OperatorProperty: semantic interface

The OperatorProperty interface consists of following several functions according to the documentation. * Infer Shapes: Still, from the perspective of a system, this function is used to allocate memory. From the viewpoint of users, this function help us to check the shape of tensors. * Request Resources: See Operator In Mxnet. It helps to access to additional space for computation. * Backward Dependency: It specify the indispensible ingredients to compute gradients for input so that make it possible for system to recycle the unnecessary ones. * In-place Option: It helps to suggest system to reuse tensors for data and gradients for element-wise operations. See Operator In Mxnet. * Expose Operator to Python: there are several functions designed for python API, like initial function, ListArguments(), ListOutputs(). See Operator In Mxnet for a detailed list and explaination.
entry.summary:
Implement ROIAlign Operation in Mxnet

Recently, I’m trying to implement ROIAlign ope

entry.title: Cython for NumPy
entry.content:
Cython for NumPy

Cython

  • A compiler

Cython is an optimizing static compiler for both the Python and the extended Cython.

  • A language

Cython is a language that makes writing C extensions for the Python language as easy as Python itself.

Speed-Up

In the later discussion, we will achieve speed-up from the following aspects. * static data type * no negative indices * no boundary check * eliminates overhead of for-loop * eliminates overhead of fuction calls: specify type of parameters and return values * efficient indexing * …

Anyway, the main ideas here are:

  • Whatever is convenient in Python may be slow in execution. We convert those part into explicit coding in C to speed up the process. (Even though always a C file is generated for .pyx file, but runing of some codes need python interpreter, which makes execution slow.)
  • Those functionalities in Python are usually more generic. So we have to provide addtional information. If the compiler lacks of some information, the codes still runs in Python way rather than C. (For newbies like me, we don’t know how much information is sufficient for cython’s optimization.)
  • In another direction, we want to make use of many useful tools already implementd in Pyhon. For instance, We want to exploit Numpy, which provides convenient API of Multi-dimensional arrays. However, note that most of time-comsuming operations of this library are implemented in C fundamentally, which means when we call those functions, we would gain little speed-up.

Steps of Using Cython

  1. write .pyx source file
  2. run Cython compiler: generate C file
  3. run C compiler: generate a compiled library(.so, a shared library)
  4. import the libaray into other modules.

It is very IMPORTANT to check whether we obtain expected elegant C codes in .c file.

Compile Libaray by setup.py

Syntax

  1. First of all, we could write any python codes. When it is possible to be converted to C implementation, it would speed up.

  2. cdef to add types for variables:

For efficient indexing, we also specify the data type and the number of dimensions for ndarray.

Note that it can only be used at the top indentation level.

  1. cdef can also be used to define functions: it is used for Cython functions that are intended to be pure ‘C’ functions. Types of parameters and return value should be specified. However, this function can not be called in Python. We are writing Python codes, but evetually it is C code.

Functions defined by def can be called direcly from Python codes. We can specify types for parameters of the function. (Return value as local variable can also be specified with a type.) However, it incurs a high overhead.

Besides, there is cpdef, which is both.

We could futher use inline for cdef function.

  1. Notice there is two type of TYPE here: one is used for compilation, another is for run-time. In those python codes we write, run-time types are used, which are exactly the same as what we used in python, e.g. np.int. Types like int (and other default types in C like unsigned int …) or np.int_t (In NumPy, suffix _t means the type is used for compilation.? I guess this is only for those default type.) are used to specify types for compilation.

ctypedef have the same usage as #typedef in C. cimport is used to import special compile-time information, such as np.int_t. It acts like #include.

  1. Specify iteration variable also help with fast loops.

We have to check whether pure C-loop is generated in the .c file.

  1. Even faster indexing:
  • using @cython.boundscheck(False) could turn off boundary check.
  • Force index variable to be unsigned int to eliminate negative indexing.
entry.summary:
Cython for NumPy

Cython

  • A compiler

Cython is a

entry.title: Tree-Based Methods
entry.content:
Tree-Based Methods

Decision Tree

Divide the feature space into multiple regions. Assign each region a value (mean value for regression)/label (majority label for classification).

Construction:

  • Binary Split to build an internal nodes. We always split at a feature leading to the most increase in some metrics.

    • Continuous Predictor
    • Discrete Predictor
  • Stop when the number of nodes in a region is smaller than a threshold, e.g. 5 observations.

  • Pruning (Regularization)

    • Pre-pruning: Stop futher spliting if increase in some metric is smaller than a threshold
    • Post-pruning: Collapse internal nodes to minimize the modified objective = the original objective + \(\alpha|T|\), after a large tree is constuctured. (Here, we talk about Cost Complexity Pruning) ### Regression Tree Train:

  • Minimize Mean Square Error

Valid:

  • Mean Square Error

Classification Tree

Train:

  • Minimize Gini Index or Entropy, which are capable of evaluating purity of a distribution

Valid:

  • Classification Error Rate

Compared with Linear Models

Advantage:

  • Even more interpretable
  • Can handle non-linear mapping
  • Mirror human’s decision making

Disadvantage:

  • Not robust. For slightly different datasets, different trees may be constructed.
  • Generally not accurate as those linear models.

Bagging

Sample several subsets from training dataset. Learn a tree for each subset. Combine predictions from those trees. Those tree can grow deep without being pruned.

Bagging will not result in overfitting.

Bootstrapping

Out-of-Bag Error Estimation

Variable Importance

Random Forest

As bagging, we build multiple trees on bootstrapped training subset.

At each split, sample \(m\) features out of total \(p\) features and restrict the spliting only happens at those features. The core idea here is to decorrelate multiple trees. If features are more correlated, we should pick smaller \(m\).

Bagging is a special case with \(m=p\).

No overfitting.

Boosting

We construct the current tree based on the residual error resulted from the previously construcred trees.

The algorithm can be described as follows:

Tunable Parameters:

  1. The shrinkage parameter \(\lambda\), a small positive number, controlling the rate at which boosting learns. A small \(\lambda\) requires more trees to achieve good performance.
  2. d splits (d=1 could work well: a stump leads to an additive model). d is the interaction depth, controling the interaction between variables (Involving at most d variables.). Small d is sufficient.
  3. B trees (may overfit, even slowly)

GDBT

XGBOOST

Boosting and Bagging are general approaches that can be applied to many statiscal methods.
entry.summary:
Tree-Based Methods

Decision Tree

Divide the feature space into multiple re

entry.title: Traditional Methods
entry.content:
Traditional Methods

Traditional (optimization) methods:

  • NOT robust.
  • Effective when appropriate.
  • two disjoint classes:
  • Only evaluate complete solutions.
    • e.g.
  • Require the evaluation of partially contructed and approximate solutions.
    • e.g.

Complete solution: all decision variables are specified.

e.g. 1111,0000,0101,1010

Partial solution:

  • an incomplete solution to the original proble

e.g. 11{00,01,10,11}, 01{00,01,10,11} * a complete solution to a reduced problem.

e.g. {00,01,10,11} | {00,01,10,11}

Additional difficulties posed by partial solutions:

  • devise a way to organize the subspaces so that they can be searched efficiently. (Tree search)
  • create a new evaluation function that can assess the quality of partial solutions. (Branch and bound)

Notice: Different representations of solutions -> different sizes of search space + potentially different manners of search (different optimization methods).

Exhaustive Search

Recall that to solve a problem algorithmatically, we have to specify following three things: 1. the representation (of solution) 2. the objective 3. the evaluation function (ordinal or numeric)

The only requirement for exhaustive search is to generate evey possible solution to the problem systematically.

We could reduce the amount of work in many ways, e.g. cut off some branches in a search tree.

Enumerate SAT, TSP, NLP (Non-Linear Programming)

In SAT, we could exhaustively search solutions by DFS.

In TSP, we have to enumerate the permutations.

  1. Mapping Between permutations and intergers
  2. Recursive generation
  3. Successive generation (e.g. in lexicographic ordering)

Notice: the specific algorithms could be checked on books and wiki.

In NLP, we use grid methods to approximate exhaustive search. The whole domain is divided into cells, the best of which could be futher divided for finer-granularity results.

Local Search

Local Search:

  1. Pick a solution as the current solution.
  2. Apply a transformation in the given set to this solution to generate a new solution
  3. If the current one is better, discard the new solution; else replace the current one with the new solution.
  4. Repeat 2. and 3. until no transformation improves the current solution.

The key lies in the type of trasformation (can be deterministic or stochastic). Two extremes are: 1. select a solution in search space uniformly at random. (Large neighbor) 2. only return the current solution. (Small neighbor)

Notice: hill climbing methods is similar to this procedure except the solution updating the current solution is required to be the best among all neighbors.

To avoid local optima, usually a large number of random initializations are required.

SAT

GSAT

Random flip a binary string.

TSP

K-opt

Change nonadjacent edges to generate new Hamiltonian cycles.

Lin-Kernighan

  • Representation of solution is based on \(\delta\)-path. Hamiltonian cycle is implied. One \(\delta\)-path could generate two possible Hamiltonian cycles.
  • Using a simple operation called switch, we could generate a new \(delta\)-path.

TODO: There are several lines in the book that are difficult to understand.

Anyway, this algorithm runs vary fast and generates near-optimal solutions (within 2 percent of optimum path).

NLP

We also consider the problem of finding the zeros of a function. It could be treated as a optimization problem. For example, we evaluate how close the result is to 0.

Bracketing Methods

  • Methods of bisecting the range.
  • Faster convergence: Regula Flasi.

an image here * Brent’s method: relies on parabolic auxilary functions (Three points rather than two points in a bracket).

Fixed-Point Methods

  • Newton’s methods: Faster, but may even fail to convege for continous functions.

  • need good initial guess.

  • the calculation of derivatives may be computationally expensive.

an image here

  • Secant methods: NOT Fixed-Point Methods! Newton’s methods without derivatives.

Gradient Methods of Minimization

  • make use of gradients.
  • May incorporate second-order derivative (Hessian Matrix) information into update. (Newton’s method)

Linear Programming

The Simplex Method

entry.title: Dynamic Programming
entry.content:
Dynamic Programming

  • Book Name: Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction
  • Authors: Richard S.Sutton, Andrew G.Barto
  • Version: Second edition, 2016
  • Chapter 4

DP algorithm are obtained by turning Bellman equaltions into update rules for improving approximations of the desired value functions.

Policy Evaluation

Policy Evaluation: given a policy \(\pi\), we compute the value functions for this policy.

The existence and uniqueness of \(v_\pi\) (\(q_\pi\)) are guaranteed as long as either \(\gamma \leq 1\) or this task is episodic(I’m not sure if my understanding is correct. See book.).

\[ \begin{align} v_\pi(s)&=\sum_a\pi(a|s)\sum_{s',r|s,a}p(s',r|s,a)[r+\gamma v_\pi(s')]\\ q_\pi(s,a)&=\sum_{s',r}p(s',r|s,a)(r+\gamma\sum_{a'}\pi(a'|s')v_\pi(s',a')) \end{align} \]

We have above Bellman equations. If the dynamic of environment is completely known, then we actually have a series of linear equations. We can solver them by linear programming, for example.

Iterative Policy Evaluation: we use the right part of equation to update the left part. The sequence \(\{v_k (q_k)\}\) will finally converge to the true value function.

Full Backup: All backups, such as in DP, are based on all possible next states rather than on sampled next states.

Algorithm: Iterative Policy Evaluation

NOTICE: We always use in-place version algorithm whenever it ensure correctness. It is usually faster, besides. We usually pick state-value function as example. But it is similar for action-value function.

Termination of algorithm:

  • obtain small differences (see \(\Delta\)) between successive estimations.
  • exceed an upper bound of the number of iterations.

Policy Improvement

How could we obtain a better policy \(\pi '\) from current policy \(\pi\), given its value function? (We think about policies that are deterministic without lose of generality: we can always find a deterministic optimal policy. It may not optimal for the real world problem but it is optimal theoretically to the MDP. Besides the following Policy Improvement Theorem carries through for the stochastic case.)

Policy Improvement Theorem: Let \(\pi',\pi\) be deterministic policies, if \[ q_\pi(s,\pi'(s))\geq v_\pi(s) \] for all \(s\in S\), then \[ v_{\pi'}(s)\geq v_\pi(s) \] for all \(s\in S\). Namely \(\pi'\) is a better policy than \(\pi\). If the inequality is satisfied strictly at any（任何一个） state in the condition, then the inequality would be satisfied at at least one state in the result.

The proof of this theorem is rather easy. We can simply expand \(q_\pi\) repeatly.

A natual choice for \(\pi'\) is the greedy policy w.r.t the value function of \(\pi\).

\[ \pi'(s)=\mathop{\arg\max}_a q_\pi(s,a)\quad (1) \]

for all \(s \in S\). This process is called policy improvement.

If we have \(v_{\pi'}(s)= v_\pi(s)\), then \(v_\pi(s)=\max_aq_\pi(s,a)\), which is the same as Bellman optimality equation. So, both \(\pi'\) and \(\pi\) are optimal policies.

Policy improvement this must give us a strictly better policy except when the original policy is already optimal.

For the stochastic case, each maximizing action can be given a portion of the probability of being selected in the new greedy policy, when there is a tie appearing in (1).

Policy Iteration

Policy Iteration: repeat

  • policy evaluation
  • policy imporvement

until policy conveges (unchanges).

Value Iteration: a Special Case of Policy Iteration

We can truncate the policy evaluation to only one sweep, instead of waiting value function to converge. The final convergence of policy is still guaranteed.

Then we have a simple backup operation that combines the policy improvement and trucated policy evaluation steps: \[ v_{k+1}(s)\leftarrow \mathop{\max}_a\sum_{s',r}p(s',r|s,a)\{r+\gamma v_k(s')\} \]

This update rule can also be understood by referencing to the Bellman optimality equation.

Besides, this update rule is identical to the one of policy evaluation except that it requires the maximum to be taken over all actions.

Termination: the same as policy evaluation.

Truncated policy iteration algorithm (including value iteration) combines multiple sweeps in policy evaluation and one sweep of policy improvement.

Asynchronous Dynamic Programming

Asynchronous DP are in-place iterative DP that are not organized in terms of systematic sweeps of the state set. A crucial requirement of convergence is that the algorithm must continue to backup the values of all the states.

It allows great flexibility in selecting states to which backup operations are applied. For example, we can focus the backups onto parts of the state sets that are most relavant to the agent.

Generalized Policy Iteration & Efficiency of Dynamic Programming

Generalized Policy Iteration: the general idea of letting policy evaluation and policy improvement processes interact, independent of the granularity and other details of the two processes.

Policy Iteration

DP algorithm can usually handle much more states (by a factor of 100) than correponding linear programming. Futher, in Problems with large state spaces, asynchronous DP methods are often preferred.

Bootstrapping: updating estimates on the basis of other estimates.
entry.summary:
Dynamic Programming

  • Book Name: Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction
  • Authors: Richa

entry.title: Finite Markov Decision Processes
entry.content:
Finite Markov Decision Processes

  • Book Name: Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction
  • Authors: Richard S.Sutton, Andrew G.Barto
  • Version: Second edition, 2016
  • Chapter 3

The Mathematical Structure of the RL Problem

The Agent-Environment Interface

RL Problem: learning from interaction to achieve goal. Interaction between:

  • agent: learner and decision maker
  • environment

over a sequence of steps.

The specification of their interface defines a particular task:

  • states: some representation of the env (Notice that this representation depends on how env looks like, how agent observes the env (probably limited) and how agent concludes the received information(see MDP).)
  • actions: The set of feasible acitons at a state, \(A(S_t)\)
  • rewards: Specified by env.

policy: Probability of actions at states s, \(\pi_t(a|s)\).

Everything inside the agent is completely known and controllable; everything outside (env) is incomepletely controllable but may or may not be complete known.

The agent affects environment through its actions.

Notice all of there is how we model the world.

Goals, Rewards and Returns

Reward hypothesis: Goals = maxmization of the expected cumulative sum of reward.

It is crucial that the rewards we set up truly indicate what we want accomplished.

  • Rewards = intermediate feedback from env.
  • Returns = the sum of rewards. (A long-run term)
  • Episodic tasks: \(G_T=R_{t+1}+R_{t+2}+...+R_T\)
  • Continuing tasks: \(G_T=R_{t+1}+\gamma R_{t+2}+\gamma^2R_{t+3}+...\) (discounted return, could be applied to episodic tasks as well.)

To unify episodic tasks and continuing tasks, we can consider episode termination to be the entering of a special absorbing state that transitions only to itself with zero rewards.

Markov Property

If the state signal has the Markov property, env’s dynamics can be defined by specifying only

\[p(s',r|s,a)=Pr\{S_{t+1}=s',R_{t+1}=r|S_t=s,A_t=a\}\quad(1)\]

State signals are a complete (and compact) summary of all history.

Markov Decision Processes (MDP)

If action and state spaces are finite, then it is called a finite MDP.

The dynamics of env as described above can be specified only by \(p(s',r|s,a)\).

Some other useful values:

  • Expected rewards for state-action pairs, \(r(s,a)\)
  • State-transition probabilities, \(p(s'|s,a)\)
  • Expected rewards for state-action-next-state triples \(r(s,a,s')\)

Transition graph

Value Functions

A policy’s value function assign to each state or state-action pair, the expected return from that state or state-action pair, given that agent uses the policy.

They are evaluated for comparing the policies.

  • State-value function \[V_{\pi}(s)=E_\pi[G_t|S_t=s]=E_\pi[\sum^\inf_{k=0}\gamma^kR_{t+1+k}|S_t=s]\]
  • Action-value function \[Q_\pi(s,a)=E_\pi[G_t|S_t=s,A_t=a]=E_\pi[\sum^\inf_{k=0}\gamma^kR_{t+1+k}|S_t=s,A_t=a]\]

They can be estimated:

  • Mento Carlo methods + average
  • Parameterization

Bellman Equation

\[ \begin{align} v_\pi(s)&=E_\pi[R_{t+1}+\gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1})|S_t=s]\\ &=\sum_a\pi(a|s)\sum_{s',r|s,a}p(s',r|s,a)[r+\gamma v_\pi(s')]\\ q_\pi(s,a)&=E_\pi[R_{t+1}+\gamma q_\pi(S_{t+1},A_{t+1})|S_t=s,A_t=a]\\ &=\sum_{s',r}p(s',r|s,a)(r+\gamma\sum_{a'}\pi(a'|s')v_\pi(s',a')) \end{align} \]

The value function is the unique solution to its Bellman equation.

Backup diagrams

Optimal Value Functions

Policy \(\pi\) is better than or equal to Policy \(\pi'\): \[v_\pi(s)\geq v_{\pi'}(s)\] for all \(s\in S\).

We have a unique optimal value function (state \(v_*\); action \(q_*\)), but possibly multiple optimal policies (\(\pi_*\)).

Bellman Optimality Equation

First of all, we pick the best action for each state. So we have: \[V_*(s)=\max_{a\in A(s)}q_{\pi_*}(s,a)\]

We have two equations as well: \[ \begin{align} v_*(s)&=\max_aE[R_{t+1}+\gamma v_*(S_{t+1})|S_t=s,A_t=a]\\ &=\max_{a\in A(s)}\sum_{s',r}p(s',r|s,a)(r+\gamma v_*(s'))\\ q_*(s,a)&=E[R_{t+1}+\gamma \max_{a'}q_*(S_{t+1},a')|S_t=s,A_t=a]\\ &=\sum_{s',r}p(s',r|s,a)[r+\gamma \max_{a'}q_*(s',a')]\\ \end{align} \]

Backup diagrams

Notice that at cost of representing a function of state-action pairs instead of just of states, the optimal action-value funciton \(q_*\) allows optimal actions to be selected without having to know anything about the env’s dynamics.
entry.summary:
Finite Markov Decision Processes

  • Book Name: Reinforcement Learning: An Introductio

entry.title: The Theory of Love
entry.content:
II. The Theory of Love

I. Love, The Answer To The Problem Of Human Existence

Separateness

Human has emerged from animal kingdom and transcended nature. As a result, he is trown out of the state of original oneness with nature. Man can only develop his reason, by finding a new harmony because the prehuman harmony is irretrievably lost.

Human is gifted with reason and is capable of awaring of itself. This awareness of himself as a separate entity, of helplessness before the forces of nature and of society, makes him desire to unite himself with men, with the world outside.

This experience of separateness is the source of all anxiety. Thus, the deepest need of human is to overcome this separateness. All man is confonted with the solution of this question: how to overcome separateness and achieve union.

The answer, to some extent, depends on the individualization reached by an individual. Infant has develop little I-ness and thus, would be satisfied with the physical presence of mother. Similarly, the human race in its infancy still feels one with nature. More intense becomes the need to find new ways of escaping separateness as human race emerges from the primary bonds.

Solutions Without Love

Orgiastic Union

Examples: * Social patterned solution: * Rituals of primitive tribes * (Close related) Sexual orgasm (e.g. Rites of communal sexual orgies) * In a non-orgiastic culture (guilt and remorse): * Alcoholism and drug addiction * Compulsive sexual orgasm (out of control)

Main Characteristics: * Intense and violent * Occur in both body and mind * Transitory and periodical

Conformity

Opposite to orgiastic union in characteristics. The most frequent solution.

Dictorial systems: * threatsand and terror -> conformity * non-conformity is possible and by no means absent.

Democratic systems: * suggestion and propaganda -> conformity * only few, like unusual herods, can be expected to refuse obedience.

Actually, people want to conform to a much higher degree than they are forced to conform.

…

Thus, it is a pseudo-unity.

Creative Acitivity

In any kind of creative work, the creating person unites himself with his material, which represents the world outside them.

In some morden jobs like clerk, little is left of this uniting quality of work. The worker becomes an appendix to the machine or to the bureaucratic organization.

But not Interpesonal!

Words and Phrases

  • instinctive: 本能的；直觉的；天生的
  • transcend: 胜过；超越
  • tear away: 逼xxx离开
  • reason: 理性
  • irretrievably: 不能挽回地；不能补救地
  • disunited: 分裂的；不和的
  • disobey
  • emancipate
  • prudish: 装正经的；过分规矩的
  • inasmuch as: 因为，由于
  • insanity
  • atonement
  • ascetic
  • renunciation: 放弃；抛弃；弃权
  • innumerable
  • individuation: 个性化
  • totem: 图腾
  • worship
  • orgiastic: 狂欢的
  • exaltation: 异常兴奋；提拔
  • transitory: 短暂的
  • trance: 恍惚；入迷
  • refuge: 避难；庇护
  • recourse: 求助；援助
  • assume a function
  • bridge the gap
  • scepticism: 怀疑论；怀疑论主义
entry.summary:
II. The Theory of Love

I. Love, The Answ

entry.title: Hello World
entry.content:
Welcome to Hexo! This is your very first post. Check documentation for more info. If you get any problems when using Hexo, you can find the answer in troubleshooting or you can ask me on GitHub.

Quick Start

Create a new post

More info: Writing

Run server

More info: Server

Generate static files

More info: Generating

Deploy to remote sites

More info: Deployment
